title: Galois Representations for the general symplectic group
speaker: Arno Kret
abstract: In a recent preprint with Sug Woo Shin (https://arxiv.org/abs/1609.04223) I construct Galois representations corresponding for cohomological cuspidal automorphic representations of general symplectic groups over totally real number fields under the local hypothesis that there is a Steinberg component. In this talk I will explain some parts of this construction that involve the eigenvariety.

proof_stages.label: Statement of the GSpin-valued Galois-representation theorem
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines GSp_{2n}, its dual GSpin_{2n+1}, Satake parameters, and states the theorem attaching a GSpin-valued Galois representation to a cohomological cuspidal automorphic representation satisfying a Steinberg condition.

proof_stages.label: Auxiliary spin-regular case and global inputs
proof_stages.summary: The first reduction adds spin regularity, interpreted as distinct Hodge--Tate weights after composing with the spin representation, and invokes Arthur’s description of the discrete spectrum together with Shimura-variety point-counting results.

proof_stages.label: Arthur transfer and Shimura spin comparison data
proof_stages.summary: Restricting the automorphic representation to Sp_{2n} and applying Arthur gives a standard Galois representation only modulo the desired GSpin lift, while Shimura-variety cohomology supplies the spin representation up to projective scalar and hence data to choose the correct lift.

proof_stages.label: Strong irreducibility upgrades local projective conjugacy
proof_stages.summary: A proposition says that strongly irreducible projective Galois representations that are locally conjugate are globally conjugate, and the Steinberg plus spin-regular hypotheses imply this strong irreducibility, completing the auxiliary case.

proof_stages.label: Plan to remove spin regularity
proof_stages.summary: The proof then aims to approximate the original non-spin-regular point by spin-regular classical points on an eigenvariety, with a later patching and base-change step needed for cases not directly covered by this eigenvariety argument.

proof_stages.label: Eigenvariety and classical points in the model case
proof_stages.summary: For a simplified case over Q, the speaker constructs an eigenvariety for a compact-at-infinity inner form of GSp_{2n}, describes its Hecke eigensystem map and dense classical subset, and chooses the point corresponding to the original automorphic representation.

proof_stages.label: Steinberg-preserving cover via standard representations and monodromy
proof_stages.summary: Because spin-regular Steinberg points are not known to be dense on the original eigenvariety, the argument builds a finite/open cover using the interpolated standard pseudocharacter, an irreducibility/Azumaya splitting locus, and analytic variation of monodromy to cut out a locus where the Steinberg condition persists densely.

proof_stages.label: Interpolation of the spin pseudocharacter and specialization
proof_stages.summary: On the Steinberg-preserving cover, spin-regular classical points are dense, so their known spin Galois representations interpolate to a spin pseudocharacter whose specialization at the chosen point gives the desired spin-composed representation, after which further work factors it through GSpin.

examples.name: Finite-image projective-representation counterexamples
examples.note: Finite-group/projective-representation examples show that elementwise projective conjugacy need not imply conjugacy of representations, motivating the strong-irreducibility hypothesis.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker cites finite-image/projective-representation counterexamples to local conjugacy implying global conjugacy, but does not construct or analyze any such example.

passes.reason: Finite-image projective representations are ordinary finite-group-theoretic examples cited to show that local/elementwise conjugacy in PGL need not imply conjugacy of representations.
Finite-image projective Galois representations factor through a finite quotient, a collapsed case, and are cited to show local elementwise conjugacy need not imply conjugacy.
Finite-image projective representations are cited as known cases where elementwise/local conjugacy does not imply conjugacy of representations.
finite-image projective representations are ordinary non-sporadic objects, and they are explicitly invoked to show local elementwise conjugacy need not imply conjugacy of projective representations
Ordinary finite-image projective representations are invoked to show that elementwise/local conjugacy in PGL need not imply conjugacy of representations.

They are explicitly cited as finite-image projective representations where elementwise/local conjugacy fails to imply conjugacy, with finite image collapsing the Galois representation through a finite quotient.

Finite-image projective representations are cited as finite-group examples locally conjugate on elements but not conjugate as representations, with finite image collapsing the l-adic representation to a finite quotient.

examples.name: Comparison groups for local conjugacy
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker contrasts groups such as SO and GL, where analogous local-conjugacy statements can hold, with Spin and PGL, where they can fail.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker only states for comparison that the implication is true or false for groups like SO, spin, PGLN, and GLN, without checking or proving it on those groups.

passes.reason: The listed classical target groups are structurally standard, and Spin/PGL_N are invoked as cases where the local-conjugacy implication fails.
The SO/Spin/PGL/GL targets are ordinary classical families used to compare the local-conjugacy implication, with the Spin/PGL failures refuting a universal version.
The classical groups are used for comparison, with Spin and PGL_N specifically illustrating failure of the local-conjugacy implication in some groups.
the named targets GL, PGL, SO, and Spin are standard classical families, with Spin and PGL cited as cases where the local-conjugacy implication fails

The groups such as GL, PGL, SO, and Spin are standard comparison families for the local-conjugacy phenomenon, not structurally sporadic or used as a single adversarial example here.
The listed classical target groups are used to compare where local-conjugacy principles hold or fail, with Spin and PGL_N serving as negative cases rather than structurally exceptional groups.
The standard classical target groups are used to compare local-conjugacy behavior, with Spin and PGL_N cited as ordinary infinite-family cases where the implication fails.

examples.name: Simplified eigenvariety case over Q
examples.note: The assumptions F=Q, l different from the Steinberg place, and semistability at l are used as a simplified setting to display the eigenvariety and monodromy interpolation argument without the full technical complications.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker specializes to the case F=Q with auxiliary hypotheses and then works through the eigenvariety construction, pseudocharacters, monodromy locus, and interpolation argument in that case.

passes.reason: The F=Q eigenvariety setup is the degree-one/base-field simplification of the totally real case, used illustratively rather than to refute a claim.
Taking F=Q is the degree-one minimal totally real base field with less arithmetic structure, used only as a simplified eigenvariety setting.
